content.text: TCO Calculator

The Azure Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Calculator estimates the 3-year cost of running your current on-premises workloads in Azure, compared to what you pay today. Unlike the Pricing Calculator — which estimates your Azure bill — the TCO Calculator models the full cost of ownership: hardware acquisition, software licences, data centre space, power, cooling, networking, and IT labour costs.

The output is designed for executive and finance audiences: it presents a side-by-side 3-year comparison with a percentage savings figure and a year-by-year cash flow chart. Most migrations show 30–70% savings over 3 years once hidden on-premises costs are accounted for.

attrs.alt: TCO Calculator cost categories: on-premises vs Azure

content.text: How the TCO Calculator Works
The calculator follows a three-step workflow:
Define workloads: enter the number and type of servers (Windows/Linux/SQL), storage capacity (HDD/SSD/tape), and database engines (SQL Server, Oracle, etc.) in your current environment.

Adjust assumptions: review and edit the default cost assumptions the calculator uses — electricity rate, data centre overhead factor, IT labour hourly rate, hardware amortisation schedule, software licences. Adjust these to match your actual environment.
View the report: a 3-year side-by-side cost comparison is generated, broken down by cost category and year. Export to Excel or PowerPoint for stakeholder presentations.

The default assumptions are conservative (industry averages). Adjusting them to your actual costs — especially IT labour rate, electricity rate, and hardware refresh cycle — will produce a more accurate estimate and often shows greater savings.
Cost Categories in the TCO Report
Category
On-Premises Inputs
Azure Equivalent
Compute
Server purchase price, refresh cycle (5yr), utilisation
VM cost at the right size, with AHB and Reserved Instances
Data Centre
Rent/sqft, power usage effectiveness (PUE), cooling
Included in Azure pricing
Networking
Switch/router hardware, WAN circuits, internet bandwidth
Azure networking (egress, VPN, ExpressRoute)
Storage
SAN/NAS hardware, tape media, backup infrastructure
Azure Blob, managed disks, Azure Backup
IT Labour
FTE hours for patching, provisioning, incident response
Reduced by Azure managed services and automation
Software Licences
Windows Server, SQL Server, antivirus, monitoring tools
AHB can reuse existing licences on Azure
Building a TCO Model Manually

The web calculator is not scriptable. For repeatable or automated TCO analysis — such as evaluating multiple migration scenarios — you can build the model in Python using the Retail Prices API for Azure costs and parameterised on-premises cost inputs.

import requests
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class OnPremServer:
    count: int
    cpu_cores: int
    ram_gb: int
    storage_tb: float
    os: str = "Windows"                # Windows or Linux
    has_sql: bool = False
    # Cost assumptions
    hardware_cost_per_server: float = 5000.0   # purchase price
    hardware_refresh_years: int = 5
    dc_cost_per_server_month: float = 150.0    # power + cooling + space
    labour_hours_per_server_month: float = 4.0
    labour_rate_per_hour: float = 85.0
    windows_licence_per_server_year: float = 700.0
    sql_licence_per_server_year: float = 3500.0

def on_prem_3yr_cost(server: OnPremServer) -> dict:
    n = server.count
    hardware = (server.hardware_cost_per_server * n
                / server.hardware_refresh_years * 3)
    dc = server.dc_cost_per_server_month * n * 36
    labour = server.labour_hours_per_server_month * server.labour_rate_per_hour * n * 36
    os_lic = (server.windows_licence_per_server_year * n * 3
              if server.os == "Windows" else 0)
    sql_lic = (server.sql_licence_per_server_year * n * 3
               if server.has_sql else 0)
    total = hardware + dc + labour + os_lic + sql_lic
    return {
        "hardware": hardware,
        "data_centre": dc,
        "labour": labour,
        "os_licences": os_lic,
        "sql_licences": sql_lic,
        "total_3yr": total,
    }

# Example: 20 Windows SQL servers
server_fleet = OnPremServer(
    count=20,
    cpu_cores=16,
    ram_gb=64,
    storage_tb=2.0,
    os="Windows",
    has_sql=True,
)

op_cost = on_prem_3yr_cost(server_fleet)
print("On-Premises 3-Year Costs:")
for k, v in op_cost.items():
    print(f"  {k:<20}: ${v:>12,.0f}")

import requests

def azure_3yr_cost(
    vm_sku: str,
    count: int,
    region: str,
    use_ahb: bool = True,
    ri_term: str = "3 Years",
) -> dict:
    """Estimate 3-year Azure VM cost using Retail Prices API."""
    url = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
    os_filter = "Linux" if use_ahb else "Windows"

    # Fetch RI price (lower than PAYG)
    ri_params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"armSkuName eq 'Standard_{vm_sku}' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Reservation' "
            f"and contains(productName, '{os_filter}') "
            f"and contains(skuName, '{ri_term}')"
        ),
    }
    ri_items = requests.get(url, params=ri_params, timeout=10).json().get("Items", [])
    ri_price_hr = ri_items[0]["retailPrice"] if ri_items else 0

    compute_3yr = ri_price_hr * 8760 * 3 * count
    storage_3yr = 150 * 36 * count  # ~$150/mo managed disk per server
    networking_3yr = 50 * 36 * count  # ~$50/mo networking per server
    support_3yr = 300 * 36  # Standard support for the subscription

    total = compute_3yr + storage_3yr + networking_3yr + support_3yr
    return {
        "compute_3yr_ri": compute_3yr,
        "storage_3yr": storage_3yr,
        "networking_3yr": networking_3yr,
        "support_3yr": support_3yr,
        "total_3yr": total,
        "ri_price_hr": ri_price_hr,
    }

# Equivalent Azure: 20x D8s_v5 (16 vCPU ≈ 16-core server), 3yr RI + AHB
az_cost = azure_3yr_cost("D8s_v5", count=20, region="eastus", use_ahb=True, ri_term="3 Years")
print("\nAzure 3-Year Costs (RI + AHB):")
for k, v in az_cost.items():
    if k != "ri_price_hr":
        print(f"  {k:<25}: ${v:>12,.0f}")

# TCO comparison
savings = op_cost["total_3yr"] - az_cost["total_3yr"]
pct = savings / op_cost["total_3yr"] * 100
print(f"\nOn-premises total: ${op_cost['total_3yr']:>12,.0f}")
print(f"Azure total:       ${az_cost['total_3yr']:>12,.0f}")
print(f"3-Year Savings:    ${savings:>12,.0f}  ({pct:.1f}%)")
Hidden On-Premises Costs the Calculator Includes
Finance teams often undercount on-premises costs by focusing only on the hardware invoice and software licences. The TCO Calculator surfaces frequently overlooked items:
Hardware refresh cycle: a server purchased for $5,000 must be replaced every 4-5 years. Amortised over 3 years, this adds significant cost per server.

Power and cooling: a modern data centre spends $0.07–$0.15/kWh on electricity for servers, plus 30-50% overhead for cooling (Power Usage Effectiveness of 1.5 is typical). A rack of servers can cost $2,000–$5,000/year in power alone.
Floor space: co-location or owned data centre space runs $500–$2,000 per rack per month in major metro areas.
IT labour: patching, capacity planning, incident response, and hardware provisioning consume 2-8 IT hours per server per month at $70–$120/hr fully-loaded labour cost.
Downtime cost: on-premises hardware failures cause unplanned downtime. At $5,000–$10,000/hour of downtime cost for a mid-size business, even one major outage per year can dwarf cloud costs.
Underutilisation: on-premises servers typically run at 15-20% average CPU utilisation. You pay for 100% but use 20% — Azure right-sizing eliminates this waste.
Interpreting the TCO Report for Stakeholders
The TCO Calculator report is most effective as a starting point for discussion, not a final answer. Use these guidelines when presenting it:
Stakeholder question
How to address it
Is this real or optimistic?
Adjust default assumptions to your actual costs. Conservative inputs build credibility.
What are we not counting?
Add migration costs (re-architecture, testing, training) separately. The TCO does not include one-time migration expenses.
What if Azure goes down?
Compare on-premises uptime SLA (typically 99.5% for self-managed) to Azure SLA (99.95%+ with availability zones).
Can we trust the labour savings?
Labour reduction comes from managed services (PaaS, serverless). IaaS lifts and shifts may not reduce labour significantly.
What about data sovereignty?
Azure has regions in most major countries; sovereign clouds (Azure Government, Germany) are available for regulated workloads.

Present TCO savings as a range (e.g. '35-55% over 3 years') rather than a single number. The lower bound uses conservative assumptions; the upper bound uses optimistic ones. A range demonstrates intellectual honesty and pre-empts challenges to the exact figure.
Right-Sizing in the TCO Model

On-premises servers are typically over-provisioned by 50–80% relative to actual peak usage, because you must buy for the worst-case peak plus headroom, without any ability to scale down. When migrating to Azure, right-sizing to actual utilisation dramatically changes the comparison.

# Right-sizing factor applied to on-premises hardware
# Typical on-prem server: 16-core, 64 GB RAM, running at 20% CPU average
# Right-sized Azure VM: 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM (D4s_v5) covers the average load
# For burst capacity, autoscale handles peaks without pre-provisioning

def rightsize_vm_sku(on_prem_cores: int, on_prem_ram_gb: int,
                     avg_cpu_pct: float, avg_ram_pct: float) -> tuple:
    """Suggest an Azure VM size based on actual utilisation."""
    needed_vcpu = max(2, int(on_prem_cores * avg_cpu_pct / 100 * 1.25))  # 25% headroom
    needed_ram_gb = max(4, int(on_prem_ram_gb * avg_ram_pct / 100 * 1.25))

    # Standard D-series vCPU:RAM ratio is 1:4
    # Round up to nearest standard size
    standard_sizes = [(2,8), (4,16), (8,32), (16,64), (32,128)]
    for vcpu, ram in standard_sizes:
        if vcpu >= needed_vcpu and ram >= needed_ram_gb:
            return vcpu, ram, f"D{vcpu}s_v5"
    return 32, 128, "D32s_v5"

# Example: 20 servers, each 16 cores / 64 GB running at 25% CPU, 40% RAM
on_prem_count = 20
on_prem_cores = 16
on_prem_ram = 64
avg_cpu = 25.0
avg_ram = 40.0

vcpu, ram, sku = rightsize_vm_sku(on_prem_cores, on_prem_ram, avg_cpu, avg_ram)
print(f"On-premises: {on_prem_cores} cores / {on_prem_ram} GB @ {avg_cpu}% CPU, {avg_ram}% RAM")
print(f"Right-sized Azure VM: {sku} ({vcpu} vCPU, {ram} GB RAM)")
print(f"Reduction factor: {on_prem_cores / vcpu:.1f}x fewer vCPUs, {on_prem_ram / ram:.1f}x less RAM")
print(f"At $0.192/hr PAYG vs right-sized ${vcpu * 0.048:.3f}/hr: {on_prem_cores/vcpu:.1f}x compute savings from right-sizing alone")

import requests

def azure_rightsized_cost(sku: str, count: int, region: str,
                           use_ahb: bool = True, ri_term: str = "3 Years") -> float:
    """Compute 3-year Azure cost for right-sized VMs with RI + AHB."""
    os_filter = "Linux" if use_ahb else "Windows"
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"armSkuName eq 'Standard_{sku}' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Reservation' "
            f"and contains(productName, '{os_filter}') "
            f"and contains(skuName, '{ri_term}')"
        ),
    }
    items = requests.get(
        "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10
    ).json().get("Items", [])
    hr_price = items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0

    compute = hr_price * 8760 * 3 * count
    storage = 100 * 36 * count   # smaller managed disk for right-sized VM
    total = compute + storage
    print(f"  {sku} x{count}: ${hr_price:.4f}/hr RI → ${compute:,.0f} compute + ${storage:,.0f} storage = ${total:,.0f} (3yr)")
    return total

# 20 right-sized D4s_v5 vs 20 over-provisioned D16s_v5
print("Right-sized (D4s_v5):")
cost_rightsized = azure_rightsized_cost("D4s_v5", 20, "eastus", use_ahb=True)
print("Lift-and-shift (D16s_v5, same cores as on-prem):")
cost_liftshift = azure_rightsized_cost("D16s_v5", 20, "eastus", use_ahb=True)
print(f"Right-sizing saves: ${cost_liftshift - cost_rightsized:,.0f} over 3 years")
Migration Cost: What the TCO Calculator Excludes
The TCO Calculator shows operational costs only. It does not model one-time migration expenses, which must be added separately to produce a complete financial picture.
Migration Cost Category
Typical Range
Notes
Assessment and planning
Azure Migrate, partner consulting, dependency mapping
Application refactoring
Depends on scope: lift-and-shift vs re-architect
Data migration
Azure Database Migration Service, downtime window planning
Testing and validation
Regression testing, performance benchmarking
Training and skilling
Azure certifications, hands-on labs, change management
Parallel running (dual cost)
1–3 months of PAYG costs
Running both on-premises and Azure during cutover period

# Add migration costs to TCO comparison
def full_tco_comparison(
    opex_3yr_onprem: float,
    opex_3yr_azure: float,
    migration_costs: dict,
) -> dict:
    """
    opex_3yr_onprem: operational 3-year cost on-premises (from TCO Calculator)
    opex_3yr_azure:  operational 3-year cost on Azure (from Pricing Calculator + RI)
    migration_costs: dict of one-time migration expense categories
    """
    total_migration = sum(migration_costs.values())
    total_azure = opex_3yr_azure + total_migration
    net_savings = opex_3yr_onprem - total_azure
    pct_savings = net_savings / opex_3yr_onprem * 100

    # Break-even: how many months until migration investment is recovered
    monthly_savings = (opex_3yr_onprem - opex_3yr_azure) / 36
    breakeven_months = total_migration / monthly_savings if monthly_savings > 0 else float("inf")

    print(f"On-premises 3yr operational: ${opex_3yr_onprem:>12,.0f}")
    print(f"Azure 3yr operational:       ${opex_3yr_azure:>12,.0f}")
    print(f"Migration one-time costs:    ${total_migration:>12,.0f}")
    print(f"  " + "  ".join([f"{k}: ${v:,.0f}" for k, v in migration_costs.items()]))
    print(f"Azure total (incl. migration): ${total_azure:>10,.0f}")
    print(f"Net 3-year savings:          ${net_savings:>12,.0f}  ({pct_savings:.1f}%)")
    print(f"Migration break-even:        {breakeven_months:.1f} months")

    return {
        "net_savings_3yr": net_savings,
        "pct_savings": pct_savings,
        "breakeven_months": breakeven_months,
    }

result = full_tco_comparison(
    opex_3yr_onprem=2_400_000,
    opex_3yr_azure=960_000,
    migration_costs={
        "assessment": 40_000,
        "refactoring": 150_000,
        "testing": 60_000,
        "training": 20_000,
        "parallel_running": 80_000,
    },
)
Using Azure Migrate for Real Cost Data

For large migrations, the Azure Migrate service collects actual performance data from your on-premises environment (CPU, RAM, disk IOPS) and produces sizing and cost recommendations based on real utilisation — far more accurate than manual estimates.

# Set up Azure Migrate project
az migrate project create \
  --resource-group rg-migration \
  --name migration-assessment-2024 \
  --location eastus

# After deploying the Azure Migrate appliance on-premises
# and running discovery (24-48 hours of data collection),
# view discovered servers
az offazure server list \
  --resource-group rg-migration \
  --site-name <site-name> \
  --output table

# View assessment results (after creating an assessment in Portal)
az migrate assessment list \
  --resource-group rg-migration \
  --project-name migration-assessment-2024 \
  --group-name all-servers \
  --output table

# Query assessment details for a specific server
az migrate assessment machine show \
  --resource-group rg-migration \
  --project-name migration-assessment-2024 \
  --group-name all-servers \
  --assessment-name perf-based-assessment \
  --machine-name <server-name> \
  --query "{
    RecommendedSize: properties.recommendedSize,
    MonthlyCost: properties.monthlyCost,
    Confidence: properties.confidenceRatingInPercentage,
    SuitabilityExplanation: properties.suitabilityExplanation
  }" \
  --output json

# Export all server assessments to CSV for Excel/Power BI analysis
az migrate assessment machine list \
  --resource-group rg-migration \
  --project-name migration-assessment-2024 \
  --group-name all-servers \
  --assessment-name perf-based-assessment \
  --query "[].{Server:name, SKU:properties.recommendedSize, Monthly:properties.monthlyCost}" \
  --output tsv > migration-cost-estimate.tsv

Azure Migrate's performance-based assessment uses 95th percentile utilisation data (not peak) by default. This avoids over-provisioning for one-off spikes. For databases, use the Database Migration Assessment tool to detect SQL Server compatibility issues and estimate Azure SQL Managed Instance sizing.
Sensitivity Analysis: Testing Assumptions

A TCO estimate is only as good as its assumptions. Build a sensitivity analysis to show stakeholders how the outcome changes if key assumptions vary — particularly IT labour rate and hardware refresh cycle, which have the largest impact.

def sensitivity_analysis(base_params: dict, variables: dict) -> None:
    """
    Run TCO comparison across a range of values for a single assumption.
    base_params: dict of all cost inputs
    variables: {param_name: [low_value, mid_value, high_value]}
    """
    def compute_savings(params):
        oprem = (params["server_count"] * params["hardware_cost"] / params["refresh_years"] * 3
                 + params["server_count"] * params["dc_cost_month"] * 36
                 + params["server_count"] * params["labour_hours_month"] * params["labour_rate"] * 36)
        azure = params["azure_monthly"] * 36 * params["server_count"]
        return (oprem - azure) / oprem * 100

    for param, values in variables.items():
        print(f"\nSensitivity: {param}")
        print(f"  {'Value':<20} {'Savings':>10}")
        print(f"  {'-'*32}")
        for val in values:
            test_params = {**base_params, param: val}
            savings = compute_savings(test_params)
            print(f"  {str(val):<20} {savings:>9.1f}%")

base = {
    "server_count": 20,
    "hardware_cost": 5000,
    "refresh_years": 5,
    "dc_cost_month": 150,
    "labour_hours_month": 4,
    "labour_rate": 85,
    "azure_monthly": 48,  # D4s_v5 RI+AHB ~$48/mo
}

sensitivity_analysis(base, {
    "labour_rate":        [55, 85, 120],
    "refresh_years":      [3, 5, 7],
    "dc_cost_month":      [80, 150, 250],
    "hardware_cost":      [3000, 5000, 8000],
})
Summary

The Azure TCO Calculator models the full 3-year cost of ownership for on-premises workloads versus Azure, including hardware, software licences, data centre, networking, and IT labour. Use it to build a financial business case for cloud migration, adjusting the default assumptions to match your actual environment. Supplement the calculator output with explicit migration costs and a realistic right-sizing analysis to produce a defensible estimate. For programmatic TCO modelling, combine the Retail Prices API for Azure costs with parameterised on-premises cost inputs to build repeatable comparison scripts.
